schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning the purity of the camp: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses, now to give the laws of the camp's purity: the unclean put out, restitution for trespass, and the law of jealousy (ch. 5, the speaking, God's command, the new section, the occasion, after the camp was ordered and the Levites set apart, now the laws that keep the camp pure, fit for God's presence in the midst, God giving these laws, showing his care that the camp where he dwells be holy, the purity that befits the presence of God among his people); the LORD speaking to Moses (the laws of the camp's purity) — the new section (the purity of the camp); the LORD spake unto Moses — the laws of purity introduced (the providence, God ordering the purity of the camp) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Keil.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying
the LORD speaking concerning the purity of the camp
God giving the laws that keep the camp pure (ch. 5)
the holiness that befits his presence among his people

schools.summary: The command to put the unclean out of the camp: 'command the children of Israel, that they put out of the camp every leper, and every one that hath an issue, and whosoever is defiled by the dead' — the three kinds of unclean — the leper, the one with an issue, and the one defiled by the dead — to be put out of the camp (the every leper, the leprosy, Leviticus 13, the every one that hath an issue, the bodily discharge, Leviticus 15, the whosoever is defiled by the dead, the defilement of death, Numbers 19:11, the figure, the unclean excluded from the camp where God dwells, the three great defilements — disease, issue, and death — figures of sin, corruption, and death, the holiness that cannot endure the unclean near the presence of God, the figure of the exclusion of the defiled, 'without are dogs... and whosoever loveth and maketh a lie,' Revelation 22:15); the leper put out (the leprosy, the figure of sin, Leviticus 13) — the exclusion (the unclean removed); 'every one that hath an issue, and whosoever is defiled by the dead' (the issue and the defilement of death, Leviticus 15; Numbers 19:11, the figure, the defilements of corruption and death excluded, Revelation 22:15, the providence, the unclean put out, the camp kept pure, vv.3-4) — Calvin, Clarke, Gill.

themes: Command the children of Israel, that they put out of the camp every leper, and every one that hath an issue, and whosoever is defiled by the dead
the command to put the unclean out of the camp
the three defilements of disease, issue, and death excluded (Leviticus 13; 15; Numbers 19:11)
figures of sin and corruption put out from the presence of God (Revelation 22:15)

schools.summary: The camp kept pure because God dwells in the midst: 'both male and female shall ye put out, without the camp shall ye put them; that they defile not their camps, in the midst whereof I dwell' — the dwelling — 'that they defile not their camps, in the midst whereof I dwell': the unclean, male and female, put outside the camp lest they defile it, for God dwelt in the midst (the witnesses noting the significance — the reason for the exclusion, God's presence in the midst of the camp, his dwelling among them demanding that the camp be pure, the lesson — the holiness that befits the dwelling place of God, the camp must be clean because the Holy One dwells there, the unclean cannot remain where God's presence is, 'I dwell in the midst' the ground of all the purity laws, the figure — the holiness of God's dwelling, the church and the believer as the temple of God, called to purity because God dwells in them, 'what agreement hath the temple of God with idols? for ye are the temple of the living God... be ye separate... and touch not the unclean thing,' 2 Corinthians 6:16-17, 'know ye not that ye are the temple of God, and that the Spirit of God dwelleth in you?' 1 Corinthians 3:16, 'follow... holiness, without which no man shall see the Lord,' Hebrews 12:14, the truth that where God dwells, holiness is required, the unclean put away from his presence, the figure of the purity that the indwelling of God demands); the unclean of both sexes put outside the camp (the exclusion, the danger of others' defilement) — the exclusion (the unclean removed); 'that they defile not their camps, in the midst whereof I dwell' (the camp kept pure because God dwells in the midst, the presence demanding purity, 1 Corinthians 3:16, the figure, the holiness of God's dwelling, 2 Corinthians 6:16-17, the providence, the camp pure, God dwelling among them) — Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

debates.question: Why are the unclean put outside the camp?

sides.position: Because God's presence is in the midst, the holiness that befits his dwelling place demanding the camp be pure (the ground of all the purity laws) - a figure of the church and the believer as the temple of God, called to purity because God dwells in them (1 Corinthians 3:16; 2 Corinthians 6:16-17; Hebrews 12:14)

themes: Both male and female shall ye put out, without the camp shall ye put them; that they defile not their camps, in the midst whereof I dwell
the camp kept pure because God dwells in the midst (the crux of the holiness of God's dwelling place)
the holiness that befits the dwelling place of God, the unclean put away from his presence
a figure of the church and believer as the temple of God, called to purity (1 Corinthians 3:16; 2 Corinthians 6:16-17; Hebrews 12:14)

schools.summary: Israel's obedience in putting out the unclean: 'and the children of Israel did so, and put them out without the camp: as the LORD spake unto Moses, so did the children of Israel' — Israel obeyed and put the unclean outside the camp, exactly as the LORD had commanded (the did so, and put them out without the camp, the obedience, the unclean removed, the as the LORD spake unto Moses, so did the children of Israel, the exact obedience, the command performed, the figure, the obedience of Israel in keeping the camp pure, doing as God commanded, the readiness to put away the unclean, the people obeying the law of purity, the doing of God's word, the refrain of obedience, 'as the LORD commanded'); Israel putting out the unclean (the obedience) — the obedience (the unclean removed); 'as the LORD spake unto Moses, so did the children of Israel' (the exact obedience, the command performed, the figure, the people doing as God commanded, the providence, the camp kept pure, the trespass laws to follow, vv.5-10) — JFB, Gill.

themes: And the children of Israel did so, and put them out without the camp: as the LORD spake unto Moses, so did the children of Israel
Israel's obedience in putting out the unclean
the people putting away the unclean as commanded
the camp kept pure in obedience to God's word

schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning restitution for trespass: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses again, now to give the law of confession and restitution for trespass against a neighbor (vv.6-10, the speaking, God's command, the new section, the occasion, after the unclean put out, now the law of making good a wrong done to another, the restitution that accompanies confession, God giving this law, showing his care for justice between man and man, the wrong against the neighbor to be confessed and recompensed); the LORD speaking to Moses (the law of restitution) — the new section (the trespass and recompense); the LORD spake unto Moses — the law of restitution introduced (the providence, God ordering justice between man and man) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying
the LORD speaking concerning restitution for trespass
God giving the law of confession and restitution (vv.6-10)
the care for justice between man and man

schools.summary: That a trespass against the neighbor is a trespass against the LORD: 'speak unto the children of Israel, When a man or woman shall commit any sin that men commit, to do a trespass against the LORD, and that person be guilty' — any sin a man or woman commits against a neighbor is a trespass against the LORD, and brings guilt (the any sin that men commit, the common sins of wrong against others, fraud, theft, deceit, Leviticus 6:2-3, the to do a trespass against the LORD, the wrong against the neighbor reckoned as against God, the that person be guilty, the guilt incurred, the figure, the truth that sin against the neighbor is sin against God, the wrong done to a man an offence against the LORD, the conscience toward God in our dealings with men, 'against thee, thee only, have I sinned,' Psalm 51:4, for all sin is finally against God, the guilt that must be owned and made good); the common sins against the neighbor (fraud, deceit, Leviticus 6:2-3) — the trespass (the wrong done); 'to do a trespass against the LORD, and that person be guilty' (the wrong against the neighbor reckoned against the LORD, the offence against God, Psalm 51:4, the figure, sin against men is sin against God, the providence, the guilt to be confessed and recompensed, vv.7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Speak unto the children of Israel, When a man or woman shall commit any sin that men commit, to do a trespass against the LORD, and that person be guilty
that a trespass against the neighbor is a trespass against the LORD
the wrong done to a man reckoned as an offence against God (Leviticus 6:2-3)
all sin finally against the LORD, the guilt that must be owned (Psalm 51:4)

schools.summary: The confession of sin and restitution with the added fifth: 'then they shall confess their sin which they have done: and he shall recompense his trespass with the principal thereof, and add unto it the fifth part thereof, and give it unto him against whom he hath trespassed' — the confession and restitution — 'they shall confess their sin... and he shall recompense his trespass with the principal thereof, and add unto it the fifth part thereof': the guilty one to confess his sin and make full restitution, repaying the principal and adding a fifth, to the one wronged (the witnesses noting the significance — the two parts of repentance toward the neighbor, the confession of the sin, the honest owning of the wrong, and the restitution, the making good of the loss with the added fifth, the lesson — that true repentance toward men is not in words only but in restitution, the wrong confessed and made good, the loss repaid with interest, the fifth added, the example of Zacchaeus, 'if I have taken any thing from any man by false accusation, I restore him fourfold,' Luke 19:8, the truth that confession without restitution is empty, that genuine repentance restores what was wrongly taken, the give it unto him against whom he hath trespassed, the restitution made to the one wronged, not merely to God, the figure — the fulness of repentance, confession joined to restitution, the wrong owned and made good with more than was taken, the conscience cleared toward both God and the neighbor, 'let him that stole steal no more: but rather let him labour,' Ephesians 4:28, the repentance that makes amends); the confession of the sin (the honest owning, the first part of repentance) — the confession (the wrong acknowledged); the restitution of the principal with the added fifth to the one wronged (Leviticus 6:5; Luke 19:8) — the figure (the wrong made good with interest, the providence, the restitution made, the case of no kinsman to follow, vv.8) — Calvin, Clarke, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the confession-and-restitution law teach about repentance?

sides.position: That true repentance toward the neighbor is not words only but restitution - the wrong confessed and made good with the added fifth (Leviticus 6:5), the conscience cleared toward both God and neighbor (the example of Zacchaeus, Luke 19:8; Ephesians 4:28)

themes: Then they shall confess their sin which they have done: and he shall recompense his trespass with the principal thereof, and add unto it the fifth part thereof, and give it unto him against whom he hath trespassed
the confession of sin and restitution with the added fifth (the crux of true repentance toward the neighbor)
the two parts of repentance: confession and restitution, not words only but the making good of the loss (Leviticus 6:5)
the conscience cleared toward both God and neighbor (Luke 19:8; Ephesians 4:28)

schools.summary: The restitution given to the priest where there is no kinsman: 'but if the man have no kinsman to recompense the trespass unto, let the trespass be recompensed unto the LORD, even to the priest; beside the ram of the atonement, whereby an atonement shall be made for him' — if the wronged man had died and had no kinsman to receive the restitution, it given to the LORD, that is, to the priest, besides the ram of atonement (the no kinsman to recompense the trespass unto, the wronged man dead, no near kin to receive, the recompensed unto the LORD, even to the priest, given to God's representative, the beside the ram of the atonement, the trespass offering still required, atonement made, the figure, the restitution that must be made even where the wronged is gone, given to God, the truth that the debt is not cancelled by the death of the one wronged but paid to God, the atonement joined to the restitution, the wrong both recompensed and atoned, Leviticus 6:6-7, the conscience cleared by both restitution and atonement); the restitution given to the priest where no kinsman (to God's representative) — the provision (the debt paid to God); 'beside the ram of the atonement, whereby an atonement shall be made for him' (the trespass offering required besides the restitution, the atonement, Leviticus 6:6-7, the figure, restitution and atonement joined, the providence, the wrong recompensed and atoned) — Calvin, Wesley, Clarke, Gill.

themes: But if the man have no kinsman to recompense the trespass unto, let the trespass be recompensed unto the LORD, even to the priest; beside the ram of the atonement, whereby an atonement shall be made for him
the restitution given to the priest where there is no kinsman
the debt not cancelled by the death of the wronged but paid to God
the trespass both recompensed and atoned (Leviticus 6:6-7)

schools.summary: The priest's portion of the holy things: 'and every offering of all the holy things of the children of Israel, which they bring unto the priest, shall be his' — the heave offerings and holy gifts brought to the priest were his portion (the every offering of all the holy things, the heave offerings, the gifts brought to the priest, the which they bring unto the priest, shall be his, the priest's portion, the figure, the priest's portion in the holy things, his living from the offerings, the truth that they who serve at the altar live of the altar, 'they which wait at the altar are partakers with the altar,' 1 Corinthians 9:13, the provision for the priests from the gifts of the people, the holy things their portion); the holy things brought to the priest (the heave offerings) — the offerings (the gifts); 'which they bring unto the priest, shall be his' (the priest's portion, his living from the offerings, 1 Corinthians 9:13, the figure, they who serve the altar live of it, the providence, the priest's portion, the rule confirmed, vv.10) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And every offering of all the holy things of the children of Israel, which they bring unto the priest, shall be his
the priest's portion of the holy things
the holy things the priest's portion, his living from the offerings
they who serve at the altar living of it (1 Corinthians 9:13)

schools.summary: The priest's right to the gifts given him: 'and every man's hallowed things shall be his: whatsoever any man giveth the priest, it shall be his' — the hallowed things a man devoted, and whatever he gave the priest, became the priest's (the every man's hallowed things shall be his, the devoted gifts the priest's, the whatsoever any man giveth the priest, it shall be his, the priest's right to the gifts, the figure, the priest's portion in the gifts of the people, the devoted things his, the provision for those who minister, the gifts given to God's servants, the truth that what is freely given to God's service belongs to his ministers, the support of the priesthood from the people's offerings); the devoted gifts the priest's (the hallowed things) — the gift (the devoted things); 'whatsoever any man giveth the priest, it shall be his' (the priest's right to the gifts, the support of the ministry, the figure, the gifts to God's servants, 1 Corinthians 9:13, the providence, the priest's portion, the law of jealousy to follow, vv.11-31) — JFB, Gill.

themes: And every man's hallowed things shall be his: whatsoever any man giveth the priest, it shall be his
the priest's right to the gifts given him
the devoted things and the gifts the priest's portion
the support of the priesthood from the people's offerings

schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning the law of jealousy: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses again, now to give the law of jealousy, the trial of the suspected wife by the bitter water (vv.12-31, the speaking, God's command, the new section, the occasion, after the laws of restitution, now the law that deals with the secret sin of adultery, where there is no witness, by an appeal to God's own judgment, the figure, God providing for the case of hidden sin and jealous suspicion, the trial that brings the secret to light, the wisdom that guards the marriage and the conscience, the matter referred to God who sees the hidden, God giving this law, showing his care for the marriage bond and his judgment that searches the secret things); the LORD speaking to Moses (the law of jealousy) — the new section (the trial of the suspected wife); the LORD spake unto Moses — the law of jealousy introduced (the providence, God providing for the case of hidden sin) — Henry, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying
the LORD speaking concerning the law of jealousy
God giving the law of the trial of the suspected wife (vv.12-31)
his care for the marriage bond and his judgment that searches the secret things

schools.summary: The case of the wife suspected of unfaithfulness: 'speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, If any man's wife go aside, and commit a trespass against him' — the case set: a wife who may have gone aside (turned from her husband) and committed a trespass against him by unfaithfulness (the if any man's wife go aside, the turning aside, the suspected unfaithfulness, the commit a trespass against him, the wrong against the husband, the breach of the marriage, the figure, the case of suspected adultery, the breach of the marriage bond, the trespass against the husband and against God, the unfaithfulness that defiles the marriage, the figure of spiritual unfaithfulness, the soul that goes aside from God, 'they... have committed adultery, and blood is in their hands,' Ezekiel 23:37, the marriage a figure of the covenant with God); the wife going aside (the suspected unfaithfulness) — the case (the turning aside); 'and commit a trespass against him' (the wrong against the husband, the breach of marriage, Ezekiel 23:37, the figure, the unfaithfulness, the spiritual adultery, the providence, the suspected wife, the jealousy to follow, vv.13-14) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, If any man's wife go aside, and commit a trespass against him
the case of the wife suspected of unfaithfulness
the suspected unfaithfulness, the breach of the marriage bond
a figure of the soul that goes aside from God (Ezekiel 23:37)

schools.summary: The hidden sin with no witness: 'and a man lie with her carnally, and it be hid from the eyes of her husband, and be kept close, and she be defiled, and there be no witness against her, neither she be taken with the manner' — the case where the adultery, if real, was hidden, with no witness and the woman not caught in the act (the it be hid from the eyes of her husband, and be kept close, the secrecy, the hidden sin, the no witness against her, neither she be taken with the manner, no proof, not caught in the act, the figure, the hidden sin that no human witness can prove, the case where only God knows the truth, the secret thing that the law cannot reach by ordinary evidence, referred to God who sees the hidden, 'there is nothing covered, that shall not be revealed,' Matthew 10:26, the sin known only to God, brought to his judgment); the hidden sin (kept close, no eyes) — the secrecy (the hidden adultery); 'there be no witness against her, neither she be taken with the manner' (no witness, not caught, the case beyond human proof, Matthew 10:26, the figure, the sin known only to God, the providence, the secret referred to God's judgment, vv.14-15) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And a man lie with her carnally, and it be hid from the eyes of her husband, and be kept close, and she be defiled, and there be no witness against her, neither she be taken with the manner
the hidden sin with no witness
the hidden sin that no human witness can prove, known only to God
referred to God who sees the secret things and brings them to light (Matthew 10:26)

schools.summary: The spirit of jealousy, whether the wife be guilty or not: 'and the spirit of jealousy come upon him, and he be jealous of his wife, and she be defiled: or if the spirit of jealousy come upon him, and he be jealous of his wife, and she be not defiled' — the husband may be seized with jealousy whether his wife was truly defiled or innocent (the the spirit of jealousy come upon him, the jealous suspicion, whether justified or not, the and she be defiled... or... she be not defiled, the two cases, the guilty and the innocent, the figure, the jealousy that may be true or false, the suspicion that the trial would resolve, the provision for both cases, the guilty exposed and the innocent cleared, the wisdom that does not leave the jealous suspicion to fester but refers it to God's judgment, the trial that vindicates or condemns); the jealousy where the wife is defiled (the true suspicion) — the jealousy (the guilty case); 'the spirit of jealousy come upon him... and she be not defiled' (the jealousy where the wife is innocent, the false suspicion, the figure, the provision for both cases, the innocent cleared, the providence, the jealousy referred to the trial, vv.15-31) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: And the spirit of jealousy come upon him, and he be jealous of his wife, and she be defiled: or if the spirit of jealousy come upon him, and he be jealous of his wife, and she be not defiled
the spirit of jealousy, whether the wife be guilty or not
the jealous suspicion whether true or false, the provision for both cases
the guilty exposed and the innocent cleared by the trial

schools.summary: The offering of jealousy, bringing iniquity to remembrance: 'then shall the man bring his wife unto the priest, and he shall bring her offering for her, the tenth part of an ephah of barley meal; he shall pour no oil upon it, nor put frankincense thereon; for it is an offering of jealousy, an offering of memorial, bringing iniquity to remembrance' — the husband brought his wife to the priest with an offering of barley meal, without oil or frankincense, an offering of jealousy that brought iniquity to remembrance (the the tenth part of an ephah of barley meal, the poor and plain offering, barley, the food of the poor and of beasts, the no oil... nor frankincense, the marks of joy and acceptance absent, for this was no joyful offering, the an offering of memorial, bringing iniquity to remembrance, the offering that called the suspected sin to mind before God, the figure, the offering that brings sin to remembrance, the plain barley without the oil of gladness or the frankincense of acceptance, for it concerned suspected sin, the contrast with the joyful offerings, this one bare and sorrowful, the figure of the offering that recalls sin rather than covering it, the suspected iniquity brought before God for judgment); the plain barley offering (the poor offering, no fine flour) — the offering (the barley meal); 'no oil... nor frankincense... an offering of memorial, bringing iniquity to remembrance' (the offering without the marks of joy, bringing the sin to remembrance, the suspected iniquity recalled before God, the figure, the offering that recalls sin, not joyful but sorrowful, the providence, the offering set, the trial to follow, vv.16-31) — Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Then shall the man bring his wife unto the priest, and he shall bring her offering for her, the tenth part of an ephah of barley meal; he shall pour no oil upon it, nor put frankincense thereon; for it is an offering of jealousy, an offering of memorial, bringing iniquity to remembrance
the offering of jealousy, bringing iniquity to remembrance
the plain barley without oil or frankincense, the offering that recalls the suspected sin
brought before God for judgment rather than covered

schools.summary: The priest setting the woman before the LORD: 'and the priest shall bring her near, and set her before the LORD' — the priest brought the suspected woman near and set her before the LORD, that the matter might be judged by God (the bring her near, the woman brought to the sanctuary, the set her before the LORD, placed before God, the appeal to his judgment, the figure, the matter referred to God, the woman set before the LORD whose eyes see the hidden, the truth that the secret thing is brought before God who judges righteously, 'all things are naked and opened unto the eyes of him with whom we have to do,' Hebrews 4:13, the appeal from human ignorance to the divine knowledge, the woman set before the Searcher of hearts); the woman brought near (to the sanctuary) — the bringing (the woman brought); 'set her before the LORD' (the woman set before God, the appeal to his judgment, Hebrews 4:13, the figure, the secret referred to the Searcher of hearts, the providence, the woman before God, the trial to proceed, vv.17-18) — JFB, Gill.

themes: And the priest shall bring her near, and set her before the LORD
the priest setting the woman before the LORD
the matter referred to God, the woman set before the LORD whose eyes see the hidden
the secret thing referred to the Searcher of hearts (Hebrews 4:13)

schools.summary: The holy water and the dust of the tabernacle floor: 'and the priest shall take holy water in an earthen vessel; and of the dust that is in the floor of the tabernacle the priest shall take, and put it into the water' — the priest took holy water in an earthen vessel and put in it dust from the tabernacle floor (the holy water in an earthen vessel, the consecrated water, the frail earthen vessel, the the dust that is in the floor of the tabernacle, the holy dust, from the place of God's presence, the put it into the water, the dust mingled with the water, the figure, the holy water and the holy dust, the elements of the trial taken from the sanctuary, the earthen vessel a figure of the frail body, 2 Corinthians 4:7, the dust of the floor where God dwelt, the humbling element, 'dust thou art,' Genesis 3:19, the water and dust made the instrument of God's judgment, the trial drawn from the holy place); the holy water in the earthen vessel (the consecrated water, 2 Corinthians 4:7) — the water (the holy water); 'the dust that is in the floor of the tabernacle... put it into the water' (the holy dust mingled, from God's dwelling, the humbling element, Genesis 3:19, the figure, the trial drawn from the holy place, the providence, the water prepared, the oath to follow, vv.18-22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Clarke, Gill.

themes: And the priest shall take holy water in an earthen vessel; and of the dust that is in the floor of the tabernacle the priest shall take, and put it into the water
the holy water and the dust of the tabernacle floor
the holy water and dust, the elements of God's judgment drawn from his dwelling
the earthen vessel of the frail body, the humbling dust (Genesis 3:19; 2 Corinthians 4:7)

schools.summary: The priest setting the woman before the LORD with the bitter water: 'and the priest shall set the woman before the LORD, and uncover the woman's head, and put the offering of memorial in her hands, which is the jealousy offering: and the priest shall have in his hand the bitter water that causeth the curse' — the setting and the bitter water — 'set the woman before the LORD, and uncover the woman's head... and the priest shall have in his hand the bitter water that causeth the curse': the woman set before the LORD, her head uncovered (a sign of shame and the laying bare of the matter), the memorial offering in her hands, and the priest held the bitter water (the witnesses noting the significance — the solemn setting of the woman before God for trial, the uncovered head a token of shame and of the matter laid open, the offering in her hands recalling the sin, the bitter water that would either curse or clear, the lesson — the trial referred to God, the bitter water made the instrument of his judgment, the water that, if she were guilty, would cause the curse, but if innocent, would do no harm, the matter left to God who alone knows the heart, the figure — God's judgment that searches the secret and brings it to light, the bitter water a figure of the searching trial of God, 'I the LORD search the heart, I try the reins,' Jeremiah 17:10, 'the word of God is... a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart,' Hebrews 4:12, the bitterness of the cup that the guilty must drink, the cup of trembling, Isaiah 51:17, the figure of the trial that vindicates the innocent and exposes the guilty, the searching judgment of God that no secret escapes, the figure of the heart laid bare before God); the uncovered head and the offering in her hands (the token of shame, the sin recalled) — the setting (the woman laid open before God); the bitter water that would cause the curse if she were guilty (the instrument of God's judgment, Jeremiah 17:10) — the figure (the searching trial of God, Hebrews 4:12, the providence, the water held, the oath to follow, vv.19-22) — Calvin, Clarke, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the bitter water of the trial signify?

sides.position: The bitter water made the instrument of God's judgment, that would curse the guilty but not harm the innocent - a figure of God's searching judgment that brings the secret to light (Jeremiah 17:10; Hebrews 4:12), the cup the guilty must drink (Isaiah 51:17), the trial that vindicates the innocent and exposes the guilty

themes: And the priest shall set the woman before the LORD, and uncover the woman's head, and put the offering of memorial in her hands, which is the jealousy offering: and the priest shall have in his hand the bitter water that causeth the curse
the priest setting the woman before the LORD with the bitter water (the crux of the trial that searches the heart)
the bitter water the instrument of God's searching judgment (Jeremiah 17:10; Hebrews 4:12)
the trial that vindicates the innocent and exposes the guilty (Isaiah 51:17)

schools.summary: The priest's oath clearing the innocent: 'and the priest shall charge her by an oath, and say unto the woman, If no man have lain with thee, and if thou hast not gone aside to uncleanness with another instead of thy husband, be thou free from this bitter water that causeth the curse' — the priest put the woman under oath: if she was innocent, she would be free from the curse of the bitter water (the charge her by an oath, the solemn oath, the if no man have lain with thee... be thou free, the innocent cleared, the water harmless to her, the figure, the provision for the innocent, the oath that clears the guiltless, the truth that the innocent need not fear the trial of God, 'if our heart condemn us not, then have we confidence toward God,' 1 John 3:21, the bitter water harmless to the clean conscience, the trial that vindicates the innocent, who is freed by the very water that would curse the guilty); the oath put to the woman (the solemn charge) — the oath (the woman charged); 'be thou free from this bitter water that causeth the curse' (the innocent freed, the water harmless to the clean, 1 John 3:21, the figure, the innocent vindicated by the trial, the providence, the innocent cleared, the curse on the guilty to follow, vv.20-22) — Wesley, Gill.

themes: And the priest shall charge her by an oath, and say unto the woman, If no man have lain with thee, and if thou hast not gone aside to uncleanness with another instead of thy husband, be thou free from this bitter water that causeth the curse
the priest's oath clearing the innocent
the provision for the innocent, the bitter water harmless to the clean conscience
the trial that vindicates the guiltless (1 John 3:21)

schools.summary: The oath against the guilty wife: 'but if thou hast gone aside to another instead of thy husband, and if thou be defiled, and some man have lain with thee beside thine husband' — the other side of the oath: if she had indeed gone aside and been defiled by another man (the if thou hast gone aside to another, the unfaithfulness, if real, the if thou be defiled, and some man have lain with thee, the adultery committed, the figure, the case of the guilty wife stated under oath, the sin named before God, the contrast with the innocent, vv.19, the guilty exposed by the same oath that clears the innocent, the secret sin owned in the oath, brought before God who judges, the warning of the curse that follows the guilt, vv.21-22); the going aside (the suspected adultery, if real) — the case (the guilty wife); 'if thou be defiled, and some man have lain with thee beside thine husband' (the adultery committed, the guilt stated under oath, the figure, the guilty exposed, the curse to follow, the providence, the guilty case, the curse pronounced, vv.21-22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: But if thou hast gone aside to another instead of thy husband, and if thou be defiled, and some man have lain with thee beside thine husband
the oath against the guilty wife
the guilty wife's sin named under oath, exposed by the same oath that clears the innocent
brought before God who judges

schools.summary: The curse pronounced on the guilty: 'then the priest shall charge the woman with an oath of cursing, and the priest shall say unto the woman, The LORD make thee a curse and an oath among thy people, when the LORD doth make thy thigh to rot, and thy belly to swell' — the priest pronounced the oath of cursing: if guilty, the LORD would make her a curse, her thigh to rot and her belly to swell (the an oath of cursing, the curse invoked on the guilty, the the LORD make thee a curse and an oath among thy people, the woman made a byword, an example, the thy thigh to rot, and thy belly to swell, the bodily sign of the guilt, the visible judgment, the figure, the curse on the guilty, the bodily judgment that exposed the hidden sin, the LORD himself the judge, the curse from God not man, the visible sign that made the secret manifest, the guilt brought to light in the body, the warning that hidden sin will be brought to open shame, 'be sure your sin will find you out,' Numbers 32:23); the oath of cursing (the curse invoked on the guilty) — the curse (the oath of cursing); 'the LORD make thee a curse... thy thigh to rot, and thy belly to swell' (the bodily judgment that exposed the guilt, the LORD the judge, Numbers 32:23, the figure, the hidden sin made manifest, the providence, the curse pronounced, the amen to follow, vv.22) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: Then the priest shall charge the woman with an oath of cursing, and the priest shall say unto the woman, The LORD make thee a curse and an oath among thy people, when the LORD doth make thy thigh to rot, and thy belly to swell
the curse pronounced on the guilty
the curse from God on the guilty, the bodily sign that exposed the secret sin
the warning that hidden sin will be brought to open shame (Numbers 32:23)

schools.summary: The curse-water and the woman's double amen: 'and this water that causeth the curse shall go into thy bowels, to make thy belly to swell, and thy thigh to rot: And the woman shall say, Amen, amen' — the priest declared that the water would work the curse in the guilty, and the woman answered 'Amen, amen' (the this water that causeth the curse shall go into thy bowels, the water working the judgment if guilty, the the woman shall say, Amen, amen, the double amen, the woman's solemn assent to the oath, the figure, the woman's own consent to the oath, taking the curse on herself if guilty, the double amen sealing the oath, the solemn submission to God's judgment, the truth that the conscience is bound by its own amen, the guilty consenting to her own judgment, the 'amen' of submission, 'let God be true, but every man a liar,' Romans 3:4, the woman placing herself under God's judgment); the water working the curse in the guilty (the bodily judgment, vv.21) — the curse-water (the judgment); 'the woman shall say, Amen, amen' (the double amen, the woman's solemn assent, taking the curse on herself if guilty, Romans 3:4, the figure, the conscience bound by its own amen, the providence, the oath sealed, the water given to drink, vv.23-24) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: And this water that causeth the curse shall go into thy bowels, to make thy belly to swell, and thy thigh to rot: And the woman shall say, Amen, amen
the curse-water and the woman's double amen
the woman's own assent to the oath, taking the curse on herself if guilty
the conscience bound by its own amen, the submission to God's judgment (Romans 3:4)

schools.summary: The curses written and blotted into the water: 'and the priest shall write these curses in a book, and he shall blot them out with the bitter water' — the priest wrote the curses on a scroll and washed (blotted) them off into the bitter water, so the words themselves entered the water (the write these curses in a book, the curses written on a scroll, the blot them out with the bitter water, washed off into the water, the words made part of the draught, the figure, the curses written and then dissolved into the water the woman would drink, the very words of the oath taken into her, the solemn making of the curse into the draught, the word and the water joined, the figure of the word of God that judges, taken in by the guilty, the curse internalized, the dreadful drinking of one's own judgment if guilty); the curses written on a scroll (the oath set down) — the writing (the curses recorded); 'blot them out with the bitter water' (the words washed into the water, the curse made part of the draught, the figure, the word and water joined, the judgment internalized, the providence, the water prepared, the woman to drink, vv.24) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: And the priest shall write these curses in a book, and he shall blot them out with the bitter water
the curses written and blotted into the water
the very words of the oath washed into the water the woman would drink
the word and the water joined, the curse taken in by the guilty

schools.summary: The woman made to drink the bitter water: 'and he shall cause the woman to drink the bitter water that causeth the curse: and the water that causeth the curse shall enter into her, and become bitter' — the woman drank the bitter water, which, if she were guilty, entered her and became bitter (worked the curse, the cause the woman to drink the bitter water, the draught given, the shall enter into her, and become bitter, the water working within, bitter to the guilty, the figure, the water entering and becoming bitter in the guilty, the judgment working within, the truth that the guilty drink their own judgment, the bitterness within, the figure of the inward working of God's judgment on the secret sin, the bitter that the guilty conscience finds, the contrast with the innocent, in whom the water did no harm, the judgment that searches within); the woman drinking the bitter water (the draught) — the drinking (the water given); 'the water that causeth the curse shall enter into her, and become bitter' (the water working within the guilty, the inward judgment, becoming bitter, the figure, the judgment working on the secret sin within, the providence, the water entering, the result to follow, vv.27-28) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And he shall cause the woman to drink the bitter water that causeth the curse: and the water that causeth the curse shall enter into her, and become bitter
the woman made to drink the bitter water
the water working within the guilty, the bitterness of the judgment on the secret sin
the inward searching of God's judgment

schools.summary: The jealousy offering waved and offered: 'then the priest shall take the jealousy offering out of the woman's hand, and shall wave the offering before the LORD, and offer it upon the altar' — the priest took the offering from the woman's hand, waved it before the LORD, and offered it on the altar (the take the jealousy offering out of the woman's hand, the offering taken, the wave the offering before the LORD, the wave offering, presented to God, the offer it upon the altar, the memorial burned, the figure, the offering waved and offered, the matter formally presented to God, the memorial of the suspected sin brought before the LORD, the trial conducted with the solemn offering, the matter committed to God's judgment with the offering, the formal presentation before God who judges); the offering waved before the LORD (presented to God) — the offering (the wave offering); 'offer it upon the altar' (the memorial burned on the altar, the offering presented, the figure, the matter committed to God, the providence, the offering offered, the handful burned, vv.26) — Gill.

schools.summary: The memorial handful burned, then the woman made to drink: 'and the priest shall take an handful of the offering, even the memorial thereof, and burn it upon the altar, and afterward shall cause the woman to drink the water' — the priest burned a handful (the memorial) of the offering on the altar, and afterward made the woman drink the water (the an handful of the offering, even the memorial thereof, the memorial portion burned, as the meal offering, Leviticus 2:2, the burn it upon the altar, the memorial offered to God, the afterward shall cause the woman to drink the water, the drinking following the offering, the figure, the memorial of the suspected sin burned before God, the matter brought to remembrance and committed to him, the offering and the drinking joined in the trial, the whole committed to God's judgment, the memorial that recalls the sin, the trial proceeding in order before the LORD); the memorial handful burned (as the meal offering, Leviticus 2:2) — the memorial (the portion burned); 'afterward shall cause the woman to drink the water' (the drinking following the offering, the trial's order, the figure, the offering and drinking joined, the providence, the trial complete, the result to follow, vv.27-28) — Gill.

themes: And the priest shall take an handful of the offering, even the memorial thereof, and burn it upon the altar, and afterward shall cause the woman to drink the water

the memorial of the suspected sin burned before God, the offering and drinking joined
the matter committed to God's judgment (Leviticus 2:2)

schools.summary: The water trying the woman, revealing the hidden sin: 'and when he hath made her to drink the water, then it shall come to pass, that, if she be defiled, and have done trespass against her husband, that the water that causeth the curse shall enter into her, and become bitter, and her belly shall swell, and her thigh shall rot: and the woman shall be a curse among her people' — the judgment on the guilty — 'if she be defiled... the water... shall enter into her, and become bitter, and her belly shall swell, and her thigh shall rot': if the woman was truly guilty, the water worked the curse, her body bearing the visible judgment, and she became a curse among her people (the witnesses noting the significance — the guilty exposed by God's judgment, the secret sin, hidden from all human eyes, brought to light by the hand of God, the bodily sign making the hidden manifest, the lesson — the searching judgment of God that no secret escapes, where no witness could prove the sin, God himself was the witness and the judge, the hidden brought to open shame, the figure — God's judgment that searches the secret things and brings them to light, 'there is nothing hid, which shall not be manifested,' Mark 4:22, 'God shall judge the secrets of men by Jesus Christ,' Romans 2:16, 'every work into judgment, with every secret thing,' Ecclesiastes 12:14, the truth that hidden sin will find the sinner out, that the guilty cannot escape the judgment of God who sees all, the bitter water a figure of the searching judgment that exposes the secret sin, the warning that no concealment avails before God, the figure of the day when the hidden things shall be revealed and judged); the water working the curse, the body bearing the visible sign of the guilt (the hidden made manifest, Mark 4:22) — the judgment (the guilty exposed); the guilty woman made a curse and a byword (the open shame, Romans 2:16) — the figure (the hidden sin brought to open judgment, the providence, the guilty exposed, the innocent cleared to follow, vv.28) — Gill.

debates.question: What does the water exposing the guilty woman signify?

sides.position: The guilty exposed by the hand of God where no human witness could prove the sin - a figure of God's searching judgment that brings the secret to light and judges the hidden (Mark 4:22; Romans 2:16; Ecclesiastes 12:14), the warning that no concealment avails before God who sees all

themes: And when he hath made her to drink the water... if she be defiled... the water that causeth the curse shall enter into her, and become bitter, and her belly shall swell, and her thigh shall rot: and the woman shall be a curse among her people
the water trying the woman, revealing the hidden sin (the crux of God's judgment that searches the secret)
God's judgment that searches the secret and brings it to light, the guilty exposed by the hand of God (Mark 4:22; Romans 2:16)
the warning that no concealment avails before God who sees all (Ecclesiastes 12:14)

schools.summary: The innocent woman cleared and blessed: 'and if the woman be not defiled, but be clean; then she shall be free, and shall conceive seed' — if the woman was innocent, the water did her no harm; she was cleared and even blessed with the conceiving of children (the if the woman be not defiled, but be clean, the innocent woman, the then she shall be free, cleared, the water harmless, the and shall conceive seed, the blessing of fruitfulness, the vindication crowned with blessing, the figure, the innocent not only cleared but blessed, the vindication crowned with fruitfulness, the truth that God not only acquits the innocent but blesses them, the guiltless vindicated and rewarded, the figure of the clean conscience that fears no judgment but is blessed, 'blessed are the pure in heart: for they shall see God,' Matthew 5:8, the innocent freed and fruitful); the innocent woman cleared (the water harmless to the clean) — the vindication (the innocent freed); 'and shall conceive seed' (the blessing of fruitfulness, the vindication crowned, Matthew 5:8, the figure, the innocent blessed, not only cleared, the providence, the innocent vindicated and blessed, the law summed up, vv.29-31) — JFB, Gill.

themes: And if the woman be not defiled, but be clean; then she shall be free, and shall conceive seed

the innocent not only cleared but blessed with fruitfulness, the vindication crowned
the clean conscience that fears no judgment but is blessed (Matthew 5:8)

schools.summary: The summary of the law of jealousy: 'this is the law of jealousies, when a wife goeth aside to another instead of her husband, and is defiled' — this summed up the law of jealousy, for the case of the wife suspected of going aside (the the law of jealousies, the gathering of the whole law, vv.12-28, the when a wife goeth aside to another instead of her husband, and is defiled, the case it provided for, the figure, the law of jealousy summed up, the provision for the suspected wife, the matter of suspected unfaithfulness referred to God's judgment, the law that guards the marriage and resolves the jealousy, the wisdom of God in providing for the secret sin and the jealous heart, the trial that vindicates or condemns); the law of jealousy summed up (vv.12-28) — the summary (the law gathered); 'when a wife goeth aside to another instead of her husband, and is defiled' (the case provided for, the suspected unfaithfulness, vv.12, the figure, the matter referred to God, the providence, the law summed up, the man's part to follow, vv.30-31) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: This is the law of jealousies, when a wife goeth aside to another instead of her husband, and is defiled

the law gathered, the case of the suspected wife provided for

schools.summary: The jealous husband bringing his wife to the trial: 'or when the spirit of jealousy cometh upon him, and he be jealous over his wife, and shall set the woman before the LORD, and the priest shall execute upon her all this law' — when jealousy seized the husband, he set his wife before the LORD, and the priest carried out the whole law upon her (the the spirit of jealousy cometh upon him, the jealous suspicion, vv.14, the set the woman before the LORD, the matter referred to God, the the priest shall execute upon her all this law, the whole trial carried out, the figure, the orderly carrying out of the trial, the matter referred to God and judged by the appointed law, the jealousy resolved by the proper procedure, not by private vengeance but by God's appointed trial, the wisdom that channels the jealous heart into the lawful trial before God); the jealousy seizing the husband (vv.14) — the jealousy (the suspicion); 'set the woman before the LORD, and the priest shall execute upon her all this law' (the whole trial carried out before God, the appointed procedure, the figure, the jealousy resolved by God's trial, not private vengeance, the providence, the law executed, the verdict to follow, vv.31) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The man guiltless and the woman bearing her iniquity: 'then shall the man be guiltless from iniquity, and this woman shall bear her iniquity' — the husband, having lawfully brought the matter to the trial, was guiltless, and the guilty woman bore her own iniquity (the the man be guiltless from iniquity, the husband cleared of wrong in bringing the trial, not guilty of false accusation if he acted lawfully, the this woman shall bear her iniquity, the guilty woman bearing her own sin and its judgment, the figure, the matter justly resolved, the man cleared and the guilty bearing her own iniquity, the truth that each bears his own sin before God, 'the soul that sinneth, it shall die,' Ezekiel 18:20, the just outcome of the trial, the innocent vindicated, the guilty judged, the husband freed from blame, the closing of the law of jealousy on the principle that the guilty bears her iniquity); the husband guiltless (cleared in bringing the lawful trial) — the verdict (the man freed from blame); 'this woman shall bear her iniquity' (the guilty woman bearing her own sin, the just judgment, Ezekiel 18:20, the figure, each bearing his own iniquity, the providence, the matter resolved, the law of jealousy closed) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: Then shall the man be guiltless from iniquity, and this woman shall bear her iniquity
